enrichment.short_description: Free accounting and invoicing software for small businesses, with paid add-ons for payments, payroll, and bookkeeping support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Wave is a cloud-based accounting, invoicing, and financial software platform built for small business owners, freelancers, and self-employed professionals. Founded in Toronto in 2009 by Kirk Simpson and James Lochrie, Wave set out to give the smallest businesses free access to tools that had traditionally been priced for larger companies. In 2019, the company was acquired by H&R Block, which continues to operate Wave as a standalone brand focused on small business financial software.

At its core, Wave offers free double-entry accounting, unlimited invoicing and estimates, and basic bookkeeping tools, all accessible through a web browser with companion mobile apps for iOS and Android. Revenue comes from optional paid services layered on top of the free product: a Pro subscription that unlocks bank-feed automation and receipt scanning, plus add-ons for online payment processing, payroll, and professional bookkeeping support delivered by Wave's in-house accountants.

This freemium structure has made Wave one of the most widely used accounting tools among microbusinesses and solo entrepreneurs who need to look professional and stay organized without committing to a monthly software budget. It trades some of the depth, integrations, and industry-specific functionality of larger platforms like QuickBooks or Xero for simplicity and cost, which is precisely the tradeoff its target audience is looking for.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Wave Is For
paragraphs: Wave is best suited to freelancers, consultants, and solo service providers who need to send professional invoices, accept payments, and track income and expenses without paying for accounting software. Its free tier covers the essentials most one-person businesses need: unlimited invoices, basic bookkeeping, and simple financial statements, making it an easy entry point for anyone just starting to formalize their finances.

It also works well for small product or service businesses with a handful of transactions per month, where Wave's bank-connection and receipt-scanning tools, available on the Pro plan, can meaningfully cut down on manual data entry. Businesses that primarily invoice clients and get paid by card or bank transfer benefit from Wave's built-in payment processing, which deposits funds directly and reconciles automatically against invoices.

Wave also appeals to budget-conscious founders and side-hustlers who want a single dashboard for cash flow, customers, and taxes without hiring a bookkeeper right away, and who may later add Wave's payroll or bookkeeping-support add-ons as their business grows rather than switching platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Wave is not the right fit for growing companies that need inventory management, project or time tracking, multi-currency accounting at scale, or detailed job costing, since these are outside its feature set. Businesses with more than a few employees, complex approval workflows, or a need for granular user permissions will also find Wave's simpler, largely single-user-oriented design limiting.

Companies that rely heavily on third-party integrations, such as e-commerce platforms, inventory systems, or CRM tools, may be frustrated by Wave's comparatively small app ecosystem next to competitors like QuickBooks Online or Xero. Wave's payroll add-on also covers a limited number of US states and Canadian provinces for full-service tax filing, so employers outside that coverage area, or with more complex payroll needs, should confirm availability before relying on it.

Businesses that expect fast, dedicated customer support at every tier may also be disappointed, since live chat and priority support are reserved for the Pro plan, and some users report slower response times on the free plan. Larger or multi-entity businesses are generally better served by a more scalable platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Wave's core feature set centers on unlimited, customizable invoicing and estimates, with automatic payment reminders, recurring billing, and a Pay Now button that lets clients pay directly from an invoice by card or bank transfer. Behind the scenes, Wave provides free double-entry bookkeeping, a chart of accounts, and standard financial reports such as profit and loss, balance sheet, and cash flow statements.

Paid add-ons extend the platform: Wave Payments processes credit card and ACH/EFT transactions at competitive per-transaction rates, Wave Payroll handles employee and contractor pay with direct deposit and tax filings in supported regions, and Wave Advisors connects users with bookkeepers and accountants for hands-on support or year-end tax preparation.

The Pro plan adds automated bank and credit card feeds via Plaid, automatic transaction categorization and merging, unlimited receipt scanning through the mobile app, and unlimited collaborator seats, while mobile apps for iOS and Android let users invoice, capture receipts, and check cash flow on the go.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Wave Experience
paragraphs: Users consistently point to Wave's clean, uncluttered interface as one of its biggest strengths, especially compared to more feature-dense competitors that can overwhelm non-accountants. Setup is fast: most small business owners can create an account, add their business details, and send their first invoice within minutes, with little to no accounting background required.

Reviews on sites like Capterra and G2 frequently praise Wave's free plan for how much value it offers, though some users note occasional syncing issues with bank feeds and want faster, more consistent customer support outside the Pro tier. Overall satisfaction tends to be highest among freelancers and very small businesses whose needs align closely with what Wave was built to do.

Because Wave is entirely cloud-based, updates roll out automatically and data stays accessible from any device with a browser, without the maintenance overhead of desktop accounting software. For businesses that outgrow it, migrating financial data to a more robust platform is a common next step, but many users report staying with Wave far longer than expected simply because it continues to meet their needs affordably.

features.title: Unlimited Invoicing
features.description: Create and send unlimited professional invoices and estimates, with recurring billing and automated payment reminders.

features.title: Online Payments
features.description: Accept credit card and bank transfer (ACH/EFT) payments directly on invoices via Wave Payments.

features.title: Double-Entry Bookkeeping
features.description: Free built-in accounting engine with a chart of accounts and standard financial statements.

features.title: Receipt Scanning
features.description: Capture and digitize receipts via the mobile app for automatic expense tracking, unlimited on the Pro plan.

features.title: Bank Connections
features.description: Automatically import and categorize transactions from connected bank and credit card accounts via Plaid (Pro plan).

features.title: Payroll
features.description: Optional add-on for running payroll, direct deposit, and tax filings in supported US states and Canadian provinces.

features.title: Financial Reporting
features.description: Generate profit and loss, balance sheet, and cash flow reports for tax time and decision-making.

features.title: Mobile Apps
features.description: iOS and Android apps for invoicing, receipt capture, and checking cash flow on the go.

features.title: Wave Advisors
features.description: Access to bookkeeping and tax support from Wave's in-house accounting professionals.

features.title: Multi-User Collaboration
features.description: Invite unlimited collaborators, such as bookkeepers or business partners, to access the account (Pro plan).

pros: Core accounting and invoicing tools are free with no user limits
Simple, intuitive interface that doesn't require accounting expertise
Built-in payment processing lets clients pay invoices instantly
Mobile apps make invoicing and receipt capture easy on the go
Optional payroll and bookkeeping add-ons let the platform grow with the business
cons: Limited third-party integrations compared to competitors like QuickBooks or Xero
No inventory management, project tracking, or job costing features
Bank feed automation and unlimited receipt scanning require the paid Pro plan
Full-service payroll tax filing is only available in select US states and Canadian provinces
Live chat and priority support are limited to Pro subscribers

faqs.question: Is Wave really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wave's Starter plan includes unlimited invoicing, estimates, and basic bookkeeping at no cost; Wave charges for optional services like payment processing fees, the Pro plan, payroll, and bookkeeping support.

faqs.question: How much does Wave Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Wave Pro costs $19 per month, or $190 per year, in the US, and adds automated bank feeds, unlimited receipt scanning, and unlimited collaborators to the free Starter plan.

faqs.question: Does Wave offer payroll?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wave Payroll is an optional add-on starting from around $25 per month, with full-service tax filing available only in certain US states and Canadian provinces; self-service payroll is available elsewhere.

faqs.question: What payment processing fees does Wave charge?
faqs.answer: Wave charges 2.9% plus $0.60 per credit card transaction on the free Starter plan, with a reduced rate for the first 10 transactions each month on Pro; bank transfer (ACH/EFT) rates are lower.

faqs.question: Is Wave available outside the US and Canada?
faqs.answer: Wave's invoicing and bookkeeping tools can be used by businesses in many countries, but payment processing and payroll features are limited primarily to US and Canadian businesses.

use_cases.title: Freelance Invoicing and Payments
use_cases.description: Send professional invoices and estimates and let clients pay instantly by card or bank transfer, with automatic reconciliation.

use_cases.title: Small Business Bookkeeping
use_cases.description: Track income and expenses, connect bank accounts, and generate the financial reports needed at tax time.

use_cases.title: Service Business Client Billing
use_cases.description: Set up recurring billing and automated late-payment reminders for ongoing client relationships.

use_cases.title: Adding Payroll as a Team Grows
use_cases.description: Layer on the payroll add-on for direct deposit and tax filings once a business starts hiring employees or contractors.
use_cases.audience: Small businesses with a few employees or contractors

features: Online payments at standard processing rates
Automated bank and credit card feeds via Plaid
Discounted payment processing on first 10 transactions/month
Full-service tax filing in supported US states and Canadian provinces

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Wave offers free accounting, invoicing, and bookkeeping software for small businesses, plus optional payments, payroll, and Pro plan add-ons.

enrichment.short_description: Weebly is a Square-owned drag-and-drop website builder for small businesses, offering templates, e-commerce tools, and a free starter plan.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start around $10/month billed annually

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Weebly is a website-building platform founded in 2007 by David Rusenko, Chris Fanini, and Dan Veltri with the goal of letting anyone publish a website without writing code. In 2018, Square (now Block, Inc.) acquired Weebly for roughly $365 million, folding its drag-and-drop editor into Square's broader commerce ecosystem alongside its point-of-sale and payments tools.

Today Weebly functions as a straightforward, what-you-see-is-what-you-get site builder: users drag elements such as text blocks, images, contact forms, and buttons onto a page and publish instantly, either on a free weebly.com subdomain or a connected custom domain. It bundles a lightweight online store, a blogging engine, and a handful of marketing add-ons into the same editor, so a single subscription can cover a brochure site, a blog, and a basic shop.

Because it now sits inside Square's product family, Weebly's checkout and card processing run on Square's payment rails, which is convenient for merchants who already use Square in a physical store but a factor to weigh for anyone who wants a different payment processor. The platform still targets the audience it always has: individuals and small businesses that want an inexpensive site up quickly rather than a highly customized, developer-built one.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Weebly Is For
paragraphs: Weebly suits solo founders, freelancers, and very small teams who need a functional website in an afternoon rather than a design system built over weeks. Its free plan and low entry-level pricing make it a reasonable starting point for hobby projects, portfolios, and local service businesses that mainly need a digital business card with contact details, hours, and a few photos.

It also works well for small merchants who already process payments through Square in-store and want their online store's transactions to reconcile with the same dashboard and reporting used for their physical location. Because Weebly's e-commerce tools cover the basics, such as product listings, inventory tracking, coupons, and shipping calculators, a shop owner selling a modest catalog can get online without learning a more complex platform.

Bloggers, community organizations, churches, and nonprofits with straightforward content needs are another good fit, since Weebly's built-in blog, simple SEO controls, and free template library handle typical use cases without requiring plugins, custom code, or a developer on retainer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Businesses that expect to scale into a large product catalog, multi-currency selling, or complex checkout logic will likely outgrow Weebly's e-commerce tools, which are noticeably lighter than dedicated platforms built specifically for online retail.

Designers and developers who want granular control over layout, custom code injection, or a large, frequently refreshed template library may find Weebly's editor and theme selection dated compared to newer builders; templates also cannot be swapped after a site is built without largely rebuilding its content and layout.

Merchants who don't want to route payments through Square, or teams that need advanced marketing automation, multi-language content management, or headless and API-first flexibility, should also look elsewhere, since Weebly is optimized for simplicity and speed rather than depth or extensibility.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Weebly offers a drag-and-drop editor paired with a library of free, mobile-responsive templates, plus native modules for a blog, contact forms, image galleries, and an online store with inventory management, discount codes, and Square-powered checkout.

Paid plans add custom domain connection, site search, password-protected pages and member areas, video backgrounds, and mobile apps for iOS and Android that let owners edit a site, check statistics, and respond to contact-form leads on the go. An App Center supplies additional third-party integrations, such as email marketing, live chat, and review widgets, for functionality not built natively into the core editor.

SEO tooling covers the fundamentals: editable meta titles and descriptions, custom page URLs, an auto-generated sitemap, and basic site analytics, giving small sites the essentials for search visibility without the deeper technical SEO controls found in more specialized content or commerce platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Weebly Experience
paragraphs: New users typically describe Weebly's editor as one of the more approachable options in the website-builder category: elements snap into place with minimal fiddling, and a working page can be live within an hour of signing up. That simplicity is also its biggest trade-off, since deep customization, such as pixel-level control or extensive custom code sections, is more limited than on code-friendlier builders.

Reviewers consistently note that Weebly's template library, while free across every plan, has not been refreshed as often as competitors', giving many sites a similar, somewhat dated look unless customized further with additional CSS. Editor performance can also feel sluggish on larger, image-heavy pages, and switching templates after launch typically means rebuilding much of the site.

Support quality varies by plan: free and entry-level users get email and chatbot help, while phone support and faster response times are reserved for the higher-tier Performance plan. Overall, the experience rewards users who want a fast, low-maintenance site over those chasing a highly bespoke design or complex functionality.

features.title: Drag-and-drop editor
features.description: A visual, WYSIWYG editor lets users place text, images, buttons, and forms anywhere on a page without writing code.

features.title: Free responsive templates
features.description: A library of mobile-responsive themes is included at no extra cost on every plan, though the selection is updated infrequently.

features.title: Built-in online store
features.description: Native e-commerce tools cover product listings, inventory tracking, coupons, digital goods, and shipping calculators for small catalogs.

features.title: Square payments integration
features.description: Checkout and card processing run through Square, letting merchants who already use Square in-store unify online and offline sales reporting.

features.title: App Center integrations
features.description: A marketplace of third-party apps adds functionality such as email marketing, live chat, and reviews that isn't built into the core editor.

features.title: Basic SEO tools
features.description: Editable meta titles, descriptions, custom URLs, and an auto-generated sitemap give small sites the essentials for search visibility.

features.title: Mobile apps for iOS and Android
features.description: Native apps let site owners edit content, check site stats, and respond to contact-form leads directly from a phone or tablet.

features.title: Blogging platform
features.description: An integrated blog module supports posts, categories, and comments alongside the main website without extra plugins.

features.title: Membership and password protection
features.description: Higher-tier plans support member areas and password-protected pages for gated or subscriber-only content.

features.title: Site statistics and analytics
features.description: Built-in stats show visitor traffic, popular pages, and store performance directly inside the Weebly dashboard.

pros: Free plan and low-cost paid tiers make it one of the more affordable full-featured site builders
Drag-and-drop editor is genuinely beginner-friendly with a short learning curve
Built-in e-commerce, blogging, and marketing tools are bundled into a single editor
Square payment integration is convenient for merchants who already use Square in-store
Mobile apps let owners manage a site and respond to leads away from a desktop
cons: Template library is smaller and less frequently refreshed than rival builders
Switching templates after launch generally requires rebuilding site content
E-commerce features are lighter than dedicated platforms for larger or complex catalogs
Faster support (phone, priority response) is limited to higher-priced plans
Payment processing is tied to Square, which limits flexibility for merchants preferring another processor

faqs.question: Is Weebly free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Weebly offers a permanent free plan that includes unlimited pages, free SSL, and basic e-commerce, though it displays Weebly/Square branding and lacks features reserved for paid tiers.

faqs.question: Does Weebly charge transaction fees on online store sales?
faqs.answer: Weebly's Free, Personal, and Professional plans apply a platform transaction fee on top of standard payment processing costs; the higher-tier Performance plan removes that platform fee.

faqs.question: Can I connect a custom domain to my Weebly site?
faqs.answer: Yes, custom domain connection is available starting on the Personal plan, and paid annual plans typically include a free domain for the first year.

faqs.question: Is Weebly good for SEO?
faqs.answer: Weebly covers core on-page SEO needs, such as editable meta titles, descriptions, and sitemaps, but offers fewer advanced technical SEO controls than platforms built specifically around content or commerce.

faqs.question: Who owns Weebly?
faqs.answer: Weebly was acquired by Square (now Block, Inc.) in 2018 and operates as part of Square's broader commerce and payments product suite.

use_cases.title: Launching a small business website quickly
use_cases.description: A local service business can build a brochure-style site with contact details, service pages, and a booking or contact form in a single afternoon.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and local entrepreneurs

use_cases.title: Selling a modest product catalog online
use_cases.description: A merchant already using Square in-store can add an online store that shares payment processing and reporting with their physical location.

use_cases.title: Publishing a portfolio or blog
use_cases.description: Freelancers and creatives can showcase work and publish regular posts using Weebly's built-in blog and gallery modules.

use_cases.title: Building a low-cost site for a nonprofit or community group
use_cases.description: Organizations with limited budgets and simple content needs can maintain an informational site without hiring a developer.

features: Basic e-commerce with platform transaction fee

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Weebly review: pricing, features, pros and cons of this Square-owned drag-and-drop website builder for small business sites and online stores.

enrichment.short_description: WeTransfer lets you send large files and share photos, videos, and documents online in seconds, no account needed for recipients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: WeTransfer is a Dutch file-transfer and sharing service founded in 2009 in Amsterdam. It built its reputation on a simple promise: let anyone send a large file to anyone else through a plain web link, without accounts, installs, or complicated settings getting in the way. That simplicity, paired with a distinctive full-screen artwork backdrop commissioned from artists and photographers, turned a basic utility into one of the more recognizable tools among creative professionals.

The core product remains a browser-based upload flow: drag files in, add an email or generate a link, and the recipient downloads directly, no sign-in required on their end. A free tier covers occasional, lower-volume sending, while paid plans raise transfer size and frequency limits, add password protection and custom expiration, and layer on cloud storage and branding options aimed at freelancers, agencies, and small teams.

WeTransfer was acquired by Bending Spoons in 2024, which was followed by a significant reduction in staff and a restructuring of the product lineup into Free, Starter, Ultimate, and Teams plans. The company has also folded in adjacent creative tools, such as its Collect moodboard app, positioning WeTransfer less as a one-off utility and more as a lightweight suite for people who move creative files for a living.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who WeTransfer Is For
paragraphs: Freelance photographers, videographers, designers, and other creatives who regularly need to deliver finished assets to clients are the clearest fit. Large image sets, video masters, and design files routinely exceed email attachment limits, and WeTransfer's link-based delivery avoids the friction of asking a client to create an account on a shared drive.

Small agencies and production teams that want a quick, branded way to hand off deliverables also benefit, since paid plans allow custom expiration windows, password protection, and a branded transfer page that looks more polished than a generic download link.

Casual, occasional senders are well served too. Anyone who needs to move a single large file, like wedding photos or a home video, without setting up a full cloud-storage account, can do so on the free tier in a couple of clicks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Organizations that need granular permissions, long-term document storage, or deep collaboration features, such as simultaneous editing, version history, or folder-level access control, are generally better served by a full cloud-storage suite like Google Drive, Dropbox, or OneDrive, which WeTransfer was never designed to replace.

Teams looking to automate file delivery through a developer integration should also look elsewhere for now, since WeTransfer's public developer API has been retired and is no longer officially supported, leaving only unmaintained community wrappers.

Anyone who needs guaranteed permanent storage or archival should avoid relying on WeTransfer transfers for that purpose, since links expire by design; the free and Starter tiers in particular are built around short-lived links rather than durable storage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, WeTransfer offers straightforward large-file sending: recipients don't need an account to download, and senders can attach a password and set an expiration window on higher plans. Paid tiers raise both the per-transfer size ceiling and the number of transfers allowed within a rolling 30-day period.

Beyond basic sending, the product includes file requests for collecting uploads from other people, transfer tracking so senders can see when a file has been opened, and branded transfer pages that let paying users apply their own look to the download experience their clients see.

WeTransfer also ships native apps, including iOS and Android mobile apps and a Mac menu-bar app, plus its Collect tool for building visual moodboards. Higher-tier plans add cloud storage for files beyond a single transfer, along with team workspaces and administrative controls on the Teams plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: The WeTransfer Experience
paragraphs: Using WeTransfer feels deliberately uncomplicated. The upload screen is dominated by rotating, full-bleed artwork from partnered artists rather than dense menus, and sending a file is typically a three-step process: add files, add an email or generate a link, and hit send.

That minimalism is both the appeal and the limitation. There is little in the way of collaborative editing, nested folder structures, or fine-grained sharing permissions, so the experience is optimized for one-directional handoff rather than ongoing teamwork inside shared files.

For the audience it targets, mostly individuals and small teams who need to move big files quickly and want the process to feel a bit more considered than a generic upload form, that trade-off tends to work in its favor, even if larger organizations may find the feature set thinner than dedicated cloud-storage platforms.

features.title: Large File Transfers
features.description: Send files without an email attachment limit; recipients download directly from a link without needing a WeTransfer account.

features.title: Password Protection
features.description: Add a password to a transfer so only people with the code can open and download the files.

features.title: Custom Link Expiration
features.description: Control how long a download link stays active, from a short default window on free accounts to longer custom periods on paid plans.

features.title: Transfer Tracking
features.description: See when a transfer has been opened or downloaded so you know a delivery has been received.

features.title: Branded Transfer Pages
features.description: Apply your own branding to the page recipients see when downloading files, available on paid tiers.

features.title: File Requests
features.description: Generate a link that lets other people upload files directly to you, useful for collecting assets or submissions.

features.title: Collect Moodboards
features.description: Save and organize images and inspiration into shareable visual boards through WeTransfer's Collect tool.

features.title: Mobile and Mac Apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps plus a Mac menu-bar app let you send files without opening a browser.

features.title: Cloud Storage
features.description: Paid plans include cloud storage for keeping and sharing files beyond a single one-off transfer.

features.title: Encrypted Transfers
features.description: Files are encrypted in transit and at rest while stored on WeTransfer's servers before download.

pros: Very simple, no-signup interface that lets recipients download without creating an account
Free tier covers occasional large-file sending without hitting typical email attachment limits
Distinctive, artist-designed visual experience that stands out from generic file-sharing tools
Paid plans scale reasonably from solo freelancers up to small collaborating teams
Native iOS, Android, and Mac apps make sending files convenient outside the browser
cons: Free plan's transfer and volume limits are easy for regular users to exceed
Files and links expire by design, so it isn't suited to long-term storage or archiving
Public developer API has been retired, limiting automation and custom integrations
Fewer collaboration and permission controls than full cloud-storage suites like Google Drive or Dropbox
Major layoffs following the 2024 Bending Spoons acquisition raise questions about long-term support pace

faqs.question: Is WeTransfer free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The free plan allows a limited number of transfers and total data within a rolling 30-day period, with no account required for recipients to download.

faqs.question: How long do WeTransfer download links stay active?
faqs.answer: On the free plan, links typically stay active for a few days; paid plans allow custom expiration windows that can be extended further.

faqs.question: Is WeTransfer secure?
faqs.answer: WeTransfer encrypts files in transit and at rest, and paid features like password protection and access controls add further security to transfers.

faqs.question: Do recipients need a WeTransfer account to download files?
faqs.answer: No. Recipients simply click the link they receive by email or that you share directly, then download the files without signing up.

faqs.question: What happened to WeTransfer's old Pro and Premium plans?
faqs.answer: Following its 2024 acquisition by Bending Spoons, WeTransfer restructured its paid plans into Starter, Ultimate, and Teams tiers, replacing the older Pro and Premium naming.

use_cases.title: Delivering finished creative work to clients
use_cases.description: Photographers, videographers, and designers send finished project files, like high-resolution images or video masters, directly to clients through a simple branded link.

use_cases.title: Sending large media files to remote collaborators
use_cases.description: Production teams move raw footage, project files, or design assets between distributed team members without email size limits.

use_cases.title: Collecting files from external contributors
use_cases.description: Teams use file request links to gather submissions, assets, or documents from clients, candidates, or vendors without granting them account access.

use_cases.title: One-off personal file sharing
use_cases.description: Individuals send large personal files, such as event photos or home videos, to family and friends without setting up a cloud-storage account.

features: Up to 10 transfers or 3GB total every 30 days
Links stay active for a few days
Up to 10 transfers or 300GB total every 30 days
Unlimited transfers up to 1TB per transfer

enrichment.seo_meta_description: WeTransfer review: pricing plans, features, pros and cons of the popular tool for sending and sharing large files online.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based employee scheduling, time tracking, and team messaging software for shift-based businesses like restaurants, retail, and hospitality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: When I Work is a cloud-based employee scheduling and time tracking platform built for shift-based businesses. Founded in 2010 and headquartered in Minneapolis, Minnesota, the company set out to replace paper schedules and spreadsheets with a simple, mobile-first system. Today it serves restaurants, retailers, healthcare facilities, and other hourly workforces that need to build schedules quickly, track worked hours accurately, and keep managers and staff connected wherever they are.

At its core, When I Work combines drag-and-drop scheduling, a GPS-enabled time clock, and built-in team messaging into a single subscription. Managers can build a week's schedule in minutes, publish it instantly to every employee's phone, and let staff handle shift swaps and open-shift requests themselves within rules the manager sets. That self-service layer is one of the platform's most consistently praised aspects among long-time users.

The product is organized around three paid tiers - Essentials, Pro, and Premium - priced per user per month, plus an optional time and attendance add-on. All plans start with a 14-day free trial that requires no credit card, and customers can change plans or seat counts at any time without a contract. This tiered structure lets a single-location coffee shop and a multi-state retail chain both find a fit, though pricing and feature depth scale with complexity.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who When I Work Is For
paragraphs: When I Work is best suited to businesses that schedule hourly employees by the shift rather than salaried staff who work fixed hours. Restaurants, cafes, retail stores, gyms, salons, hotels, and other hospitality or service operations are the platform's core audience, since these industries depend on getting the right number of people on the floor at the right time.

The tool also fits growing multi-location operators. The Pro and Premium tiers add labor sharing across sites, forecasting tools, custom reporting, and role-based permissions, which help area or regional managers oversee several locations without losing visibility into any single one. Franchise owners and small regional chains frequently cite this multi-site view as a reason they moved off spreadsheets.

Small business owners scheduling for the first time will also find it approachable. The interface is simple enough that a manager with no scheduling-software background can build a working week within an afternoon, and the mobile apps mean staff can check schedules, request time off, and swap shifts without a manager fielding every text message.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Companies with mostly salaried, fixed-schedule staff - professional services firms, most software companies, or back-office teams - will not get much value from a tool purpose-built around shift rotation, availability, and hourly time tracking. A general-purpose HR or project time-tracking tool is usually a better fit for that kind of workforce.

Field teams that regularly work without reliable internet access should also be cautious, since the mobile app does not fully function offline; punches and schedule changes typically need a connection to sync reliably. Businesses that need deep integration with a specific existing HRIS or a highly specialized payroll workflow beyond the native integrations should confirm compatibility first, since deeper connections are generally Premium-plan or add-on features.

Some current users also report occasional bugs, syncing delays, and notification issues on both the web portal and mobile app, along with a learning curve around the more advanced permission and forecasting features. Organizations with zero tolerance for occasional glitches, or that need highly customized enterprise-grade workflows out of the box, may want to weigh dedicated workforce-management suites alongside When I Work before deciding.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: The scheduling engine is the heart of the product: drag-and-drop shift building, shift templates, an auto-scheduler that matches shifts to availability and preferences, and open shifts that employees can claim themselves. Shift swap and drop requests route through manager approval rules automatically, cutting down the back-and-forth that usually happens over text or phone calls.

The built-in time clock lets employees clock in and out from their phone or a shared kiosk, with GPS location capture and geofencing that confirms staff are on-site when they punch in. Time and attendance data feeds directly into native payroll integrations with providers like Gusto, QuickBooks Online, ADP Workforce Now, and Square Payroll, reducing manual re-entry when running payroll.

Team communication is built in rather than bolted on, with one-to-one and group messaging, shift reminders, and announcements delivered through the same app employees already use for their schedule. Higher tiers add labor cost forecasting, custom and 24-hour callout reporting, role-based permissions for multi-location oversight, and, on the Premium plan, API access, webhooks, and SAML/SSO for larger organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: The When I Work Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, When I Work is built around the mobile app as much as the web dashboard - most employees interact with their schedule, time clock, and messages entirely from their phone, while managers do the heavier schedule-building from a browser. The overall workflow favors speed: publishing a schedule, approving a swap, or answering a time-off request typically takes a few taps rather than a multi-step process.

Onboarding a single location is generally described as fast, with new managers able to import staff, set availability rules, and publish a first schedule within a day. Multi-location or Premium-tier setups involving forecasting, custom permissions, or SSO take more configuration, and larger teams should expect a longer ramp-up period as they tune the rules that govern auto-scheduling and reporting.

Support is available through an online help center and ticket-based customer care in English, and the company continues to ship incremental feature updates rather than major overhauls. For shift-based teams that want scheduling, time tracking, and messaging bundled into one subscription instead of three separate tools, the day-to-day experience is generally straightforward, even though some users note occasional bugs and sync delays that the product continues to work through.

features.title: Drag-and-Drop Scheduling
features.description: Build, edit, and publish weekly or multi-week schedules quickly using a visual drag-and-drop grid and reusable shift templates.

features.title: Auto-Scheduler
features.description: Automatically matches open shifts to employees based on their availability, eligibility, and stated shift preferences.

features.title: GPS Time Clock & Geofencing
features.description: Employees clock in and out from their phone or a shared kiosk, with GPS capture and geofencing to confirm on-site punches.

features.title: Shift Swapping & Open Shifts
features.description: Staff can request swaps, drop shifts, or claim open shifts themselves, with manager approval rules applied automatically.

features.title: Team Messaging
features.description: Built-in one-to-one and group messaging, plus shift reminders and announcements, delivered through the same scheduling app.

features.title: Time-Off & Availability Management
features.description: Employees submit availability and time-off requests that managers can review and approve directly within the schedule.

features.title: Labor Forecasting & Reporting
features.description: Pro and Premium plans add labor cost forecasting, custom reports, and 24-hour callout reporting for tighter budget control.

features.title: Payroll Integrations
features.description: Native integrations with providers such as Gusto, QuickBooks Online, ADP Workforce Now, and Square Payroll streamline payroll runs.

features.title: Mobile Apps for iOS & Android
features.description: Full-featured native apps let employees and managers handle scheduling, time tracking, and messaging from anywhere.

features.title: API, Webhooks & SSO
features.description: The Premium plan adds API key access, webhooks, and SAML/SSO for organizations that need deeper system integration.

pros: Fast, intuitive drag-and-drop schedule building that most managers pick up quickly
Employees can self-serve shift swaps, drops, and open-shift claims within manager-set rules
GPS-verified time clock ties directly into several native payroll integrations
Team messaging, reminders, and schedule access are combined in one mobile app
Transparent per-user monthly pricing with no long-term contract and easy plan changes
cons: The mobile app does not fully work offline, which is a problem for job sites with poor connectivity
Some users report bugs, crashes, and syncing delays on both web and mobile
Advanced features like forecasting, custom permissions, and SSO are gated behind higher-priced tiers
Time Clock & Attendance is a separate paid add-on and its pricing is not published upfront
Best suited to hourly, shift-based work, so salaried or fixed-schedule teams get less value

faqs.question: Does When I Work have a free plan?
faqs.answer: There is no permanent free plan. When I Work offers a 14-day free trial with full feature access and no credit card required, after which you choose a paid plan.

faqs.question: How much does When I Work cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans are billed per user per month: Essentials at $2.50, Pro at $5, and Premium at $8. Time Clock & Attendance is an optional add-on with pricing available on request, and custom enterprise pricing is also available.

faqs.question: Does When I Work integrate with payroll software?
faqs.answer: Yes. With the Time Clock & Attendance add-on enabled, When I Work integrates natively with providers including Gusto, QuickBooks Online, ADP Workforce Now, and Square Payroll.

faqs.question: Is there a When I Work mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, When I Work has native iOS and Android apps that let employees view schedules, clock in and out, message coworkers, and request time off or shift swaps.

faqs.question: What types of businesses use When I Work?
faqs.answer: It is designed for shift-based, hourly workforces such as restaurants, retail stores, hospitality, healthcare, gyms, and other service businesses with rotating schedules.

use_cases.title: Restaurant and hospitality shift scheduling
use_cases.description: Restaurant, cafe, and hotel managers use When I Work to build weekly shift schedules, fill last-minute open shifts, and communicate changes instantly to front- and back-of-house staff.

use_cases.title: Multi-location retail workforce management
use_cases.description: Retail chains use labor sharing, forecasting, and custom reporting on the Pro and Premium tiers to give regional managers visibility across several store locations from one account.

use_cases.title: Healthcare and home-care staff scheduling
use_cases.description: Clinics, home-care agencies, and other healthcare providers use GPS-verified time tracking and shift scheduling to confirm caregiver attendance and simplify payroll for hourly staff.

use_cases.title: Replacing spreadsheet-based scheduling
use_cases.description: Small business owners moving off spreadsheets or paper schedules use the Essentials plan to publish schedules, track time, and message staff without hiring dedicated scheduling admin support.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and first-time schedulers

features: Advanced customization for complex, scaled operations

enrichment.seo_meta_description: When I Work review: pricing, features, pros & cons of this employee scheduling, time tracking, and team messaging app for shift-based businesses.

enrichment.short_description: Whereby offers browser-based video meetings with no downloads, custom-branded rooms, recording, transcription, and an embeddable video calling API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Whereby is a browser-based video conferencing platform built on WebRTC, letting anyone join a meeting through a simple link with no app or plugin to install. It traces its roots to appear.in, a project born inside Norwegian telecom Telenor in 2013 that pioneered peer-to-peer video calling directly in the browser. The company spun out, was acquired by Videonor, and rebranded as Whereby in 2019, before Whereby itself became part of video infrastructure provider Pexip in 2021.

Today Whereby operates two related products: Whereby Meetings, a consumer-facing app for scheduling and hosting persistent video rooms, and Whereby Embedded, a video calling API and SDK that lets developers bake Whereby's video technology directly into their own products. Both share the same emphasis on frictionless, no-download video built for the modern web rather than legacy desktop clients.

The company is headquartered in Norway and remains a relatively small, focused team compared to giants like Zoom or Microsoft Teams. Rather than chasing every enterprise collaboration feature, Whereby has stayed narrowly focused on making one-click, browser-native video calling as simple, fast, and pleasant to use as possible for small teams, freelancers, and platforms that need embedded video. Whereby has also leaned into browser compatibility across Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge, and maintains lightweight iOS and Android apps for joining calls on mobile devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Whereby Is For
paragraphs: Whereby is best suited to freelancers, consultants, coaches, and small businesses that need a professional, branded meeting room without asking clients to download software or create an account. A single persistent room URL that can be reused for every meeting is a natural fit for recurring client calls, therapy or coaching sessions, and sales demos.

Small and mid-sized teams that want lightweight video calling alongside chat, screen sharing, and light collaboration tools (like embedded Miro boards or Google Docs) will also find Whereby a good match, especially teams that value simplicity over a deep enterprise feature set. It also suits distributed and remote-first companies that prioritize quick, ad hoc video huddles over scheduled, agenda-heavy conferences. Telehealth providers, online tutors, and recruiters running back-to-back interviews are especially common use cases, since a single reusable link removes the friction of sending a new invite for every session.

Developers and product teams building their own apps, marketplaces, telehealth platforms, or online learning tools are a distinct and important audience: Whereby Embedded gives them a video calling API and SDK so they can add real-time video to their own product without building WebRTC infrastructure from scratch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Large enterprises that run frequent town halls, webinars, or trainings for hundreds or thousands of attendees should look elsewhere, since Whereby Meetings caps room capacity well below what dedicated webinar platforms support. Organizations with strict, complex IT governance needs, such as enterprise-wide SSO rollouts, granular device management, or deep integration with an existing Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace identity stack, may find Whereby's admin tooling comparatively light.

Teams that rely heavily on phone dial-in for international participants may also be frustrated, since Whereby's dial-in support is limited primarily to US numbers. Similarly, organizations needing a fully multilingual meeting interface for global, non-English-speaking staff should verify current language coverage before committing, since Whereby's own apps are documented primarily in English. Companies bound by regional data residency rules that require infrastructure outside Whereby's supported regions should confirm compliance details directly with the vendor before adopting it at scale.

Finally, businesses that want a single all-in-one suite combining video, chat, file storage, and productivity apps under one roof, in the style of Microsoft Teams or Google Meet bundled with a broader workspace, will likely find Whereby too narrowly scoped, since it focuses specifically on video calling rather than being a complete collaboration platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Whereby offers persistent, link-based meeting rooms with HD video, screen sharing, and a customizable grid or spotlight layout, along with host controls like locked rooms, waiting areas, and the ability to mute or remove participants. Meetings can be recorded to the cloud or locally, and both live captioning and post-call transcription are available to improve accessibility and make meetings easier to reference later.

Collaboration features extend the core call: breakout groups for splitting participants into smaller discussions, embedded Miro whiteboards, real-time Google Docs collaboration, in-call chat with reactions, and drag-and-drop file sharing. Paid plans add custom branding, custom subdomains, and unlimited or additional room URLs, so a business can present a consistent, professional look across every meeting link it shares.

For developers, Whereby Embedded exposes the same underlying video infrastructure as an API and SDK, enabling video calling to be embedded inside a website, mobile app, or SaaS product. On the security side, Whereby supports end-to-end encryption for peer-to-peer calls, is GDPR compliant and ISO 27001 certified, and can be configured to support HIPAA requirements for healthcare use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Whereby Experience
paragraphs: The defining characteristic of using Whereby is how little friction stands between clicking a link and being on a call. There is no mandatory account creation for guests and no software to install, so joining feels closer to opening a web page than launching a conferencing app. Reviewers consistently point to this simplicity, along with a clean, modern interface, as Whereby's biggest strength relative to heavier competitors.

That simplicity comes with tradeoffs. Because rooms are the core unit rather than scheduled, calendar-driven meetings in the style of Zoom or Teams, teams that rely heavily on complex meeting scheduling workflows may need to adapt their habits. Attendee caps, currently up to 200 participants on the highest self-serve tier, mean Whereby is built for team and small-group meetings rather than large broadcast-style events.

Overall, the experience skews toward speed and ease over configurability. For its core audience, that tradeoff is the point: fewer settings to manage, less onboarding friction for guests, and a video calling tool that gets out of the way so the conversation stays the focus.

features.title: No-download browser calling
features.description: Meetings run entirely in the browser using WebRTC, so guests can join from a link without installing any app or creating an account.

features.title: Persistent room URLs
features.description: Each room has a reusable link that can be shared repeatedly, making it easy to maintain one consistent meeting address for recurring calls.

features.title: Screen sharing and spotlight layouts
features.description: Presenters can share their screen and adjust the video grid or spotlight a speaker so the right content stays in focus.

features.title: Cloud and local recording
features.description: Calls can be recorded to Whereby's cloud storage or saved locally, with transcription and live captions available for accessibility.

features.title: Breakout groups
features.description: Hosts can split larger meetings into smaller breakout sessions for discussions before bringing everyone back together.

features.title: Embedded collaboration tools
features.description: Built-in Miro whiteboards and real-time Google Docs collaboration let participants work together without leaving the call.

features.title: Custom branding and subdomains
features.description: Paid plans let businesses add their own logo, colors, and a custom subdomain so meeting links look and feel on-brand.

features.title: Whereby Embedded API and SDK
features.description: Developers can integrate Whereby's video calling technology directly into their own websites, apps, or platforms via API and SDK.

features.title: Security and compliance
features.description: End-to-end encryption for peer-to-peer calls, GDPR compliance, ISO 27001 certification, and HIPAA-configurable options support regulated use cases.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Lightweight iOS and Android apps let participants join and host meetings from mobile devices in addition to desktop browsers.

pros: Guests can join instantly from a browser link with no downloads or account creation required
Simple, clean interface that reviewers consistently describe as easy to learn and use
Persistent, reusable room URLs are convenient for recurring client or team meetings
Offers a genuine free plan plus an API product for developers who want to embed video
Solid security posture with GDPR compliance, ISO 27001 certification, and HIPAA-configurable options
cons: Free plan is limited to short calls and a single room, which may feel restrictive quickly
Attendee caps make it a poor fit for large webinars, town halls, or broadcast-style events
Phone dial-in support is limited primarily to US numbers
Fewer deep enterprise admin, identity, and governance controls than larger platforms like Zoom or Teams
Primarily English-language interface, which may not suit fully multilingual organizations

faqs.question: Do meeting guests need to download an app or create an account to join a Whereby call?
faqs.answer: No. Whereby runs in the browser using WebRTC, so guests can join a meeting by simply clicking a room link, with no software installation or account required.

faqs.question: Is Whereby free to use?
faqs.answer: Whereby offers a free plan with one host, one room, and calls limited to a small group and short duration. Paid Pro and Business plans remove those limits and add more rooms, larger meeting capacity, and custom branding.

faqs.question: What is Whereby Embedded?
faqs.answer: Whereby Embedded is a separate API and SDK product that lets developers integrate Whereby's video calling technology directly into their own websites, apps, or platforms rather than using the standalone Whereby Meetings app.

faqs.question: How many people can join a Whereby meeting?
faqs.answer: Capacity depends on the plan, ranging from a handful of participants on the free tier up to around 200 attendees on paid Meetings plans, with additional viewer-only capacity available on higher tiers.

faqs.question: Is Whereby secure and compliant for sensitive conversations?
faqs.answer: Whereby supports end-to-end encryption for peer-to-peer calls, is GDPR compliant, holds ISO 27001 certification, and offers HIPAA-configurable settings, making it usable for many healthcare and business compliance needs.

use_cases.title: Client meetings for freelancers and consultants
use_cases.description: A reusable, branded room link makes it simple to host recurring client calls, coaching sessions, or consultations without asking clients to install anything.

use_cases.title: Lightweight team video calls
use_cases.description: Small and distributed teams use Whereby for quick huddles, standups, and ad hoc video chats that don't require heavyweight scheduling or a full collaboration suite.

use_cases.title: Telehealth and virtual appointments
use_cases.description: A persistent, easy-to-join room supports one-on-one virtual visits between practitioners and clients, with security and compliance features suited to sensitive conversations.

use_cases.title: Embedded video in custom applications
use_cases.description: Whereby Embedded lets product and engineering teams add live video calling into their own marketplace, learning platform, or SaaS product using an API and SDK.

features: Basic video conferencing and screen sharing
Shared and flex rooms with a custom subdomain

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Whereby review: browser-based video meetings with no downloads, custom-branded rooms, recording, and an embeddable video API. Pricing, features, pros and cons.

enrichment.short_description: AI-native code editor with the Cascade agent for multi-file edits, terminal automation, and fast autocomplete; now rebranded as Devin Desktop.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Windsurf is an AI-native code editor built on a Visual Studio Code fork by the team formerly known as Codeium, best known for its Cascade agent: an AI pane that can read an entire codebase, plan multi-step changes, edit multiple files at once, run terminal commands, and call external tools through the Model Context Protocol, all while staging its work as a reviewable diff before anything is written to disk. Alongside Cascade, the editor ships fast inline autocomplete that predicts entire edits rather than single lines, plus support for several frontier models including Anthropic's Claude family, OpenAI's GPT models, and Windsurf's own in-house SWE coding model.

The company was founded in 2021 in Mountain View, California, initially as Codeium, an AI-assisted coding startup that built autocomplete and chat tools for existing editors before launching its own standalone IDE under the Windsurf name. In 2025, Windsurf was acquired by Cognition, the company behind the autonomous coding agent Devin, in a deal reported at roughly 250 million dollars. In June 2026, Cognition folded the product into its Devin lineup, renaming it Devin Desktop as part of a broader push to unify its supervised (desktop) and autonomous (cloud) coding agents under one brand; existing Windsurf accounts, plans, and settings carried over automatically.

For a directory entry, Windsurf is best understood as the editor's enduring identity among developers even after the rename: it is the product people search for, recommend, and compare against Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and other AI-assisted IDEs, and its documentation, extensions, and enterprise login still live at windsurf.com/enterprise and related subdomains.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Windsurf Is For
paragraphs: Windsurf suits professional developers and teams who want an AI agent embedded directly in their editor rather than bolted on as a chat sidebar. It works well for engineers tackling multi-file refactors, migrations, or feature builds where an agent that can plan, edit, run commands, and iterate saves real time, especially when paired with the diff-review workflow that keeps a human in the loop before code lands.

Startups and small teams that want to move fast benefit from the generous free tier for autocomplete and light agent use, then scale into a paid plan as usage grows. Engineering teams that already lean on Model Context Protocol servers, internal tooling, or a mix of AI models will appreciate Cascade's flexibility in connecting external context sources and switching between Claude, GPT, and Windsurf's own models depending on the task.

Enterprises with existing Codeium or Windsurf deployments, including those using self-hosted or air-gapped setups, are a natural fit given the product's roots in enterprise-focused code assistance, and its continuity of plans and licensing through the Cognition acquisition and the Devin Desktop rename.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Developers who want a single, stable brand and interface that will not change again soon should be cautious: Windsurf has been renamed once already (from Codeium) and again in 2026 (to Devin Desktop), and its pricing model has also shifted more than once, from credit-based billing to daily and weekly quotas. Anyone allergic to that kind of platform churn may prefer a tool with a longer track record of naming and pricing stability.

Organizations with strict requirements around vendor independence or long-term roadmap certainty should also weigh the fact that Windsurf now operates as a subsidiary brand inside Cognition's Devin product family rather than as a standalone company, which means its future direction is tied to Cognition's broader agent strategy rather than a dedicated Windsurf roadmap.

Developers who prefer minimal, manual-first editors without an AI agent constantly proposing changes, or who need deep parity with the full Visual Studio Code extension marketplace, may find the agent-centric workflow and editor fork model more friction than benefit compared with using a thin AI plugin on top of standard VS Code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the center of the product is Cascade, the agentic pane that turns natural-language requests into concrete code changes: it reads the project, forms a plan, edits the relevant files, and can run shell commands such as installing dependencies or running migrations, surfacing every tool call, command, and file change as an inspectable step in the conversation.

Autocomplete is handled separately and is fast enough to feel like traditional IDE completion while still predicting larger, multi-line edits and next likely actions across a file, and it remains available even on the free tier without the quota limits that apply to agent tasks.

Model Context Protocol support lets teams plug in their own external tools and data sources, so Cascade can pull context from internal systems, documentation, or other services rather than being limited to what is already open in the editor, and the editor supports multiple underlying models so teams can choose between third-party frontier models and Windsurf's own coding-focused model depending on cost and task complexity.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Windsurf Experience
paragraphs: Using Windsurf feels like working inside a familiar VS Code-style editor with an AI collaborator built into the core workflow rather than added as an afterthought: prompts, diffs, and terminal output sit alongside the file tree and editor panes, and changes are proposed as staged diffs that a developer approves, rejects, or edits before they are committed to the codebase.

The day-to-day rhythm centers on describing a task in plain language, watching Cascade break it into steps, and reviewing each file change or command as it happens, which keeps the pace of AI-assisted development fast without removing the developer from the decision-making loop.

Following the Cognition acquisition and the mid-2026 rename to Devin Desktop, the underlying day-to-day experience for existing users has largely continued as before, with the same interface, keybindings, and plans carrying over; the most visible change for long-time Windsurf users has been the branding shift and the product's positioning as one part of a larger family of Devin coding agents rather than a standalone editor.

features.title: Cascade AI Agent
features.description: An agentic pane that reads your codebase, plans multi-step changes, edits multiple files, and runs terminal commands in one workflow.

features.title: Reviewable Diffs
features.description: Cascade stages every edit as a diff with per-step approval so changes can be reviewed, edited, or rejected before they hit disk.

features.title: Fast Inline Autocomplete
features.description: Predictive completion that suggests full edits and next likely actions, not just single lines, and remains unlimited on the free tier.

features.title: Model Context Protocol Support
features.description: Connect external tools, internal systems, and data sources directly into Cascade's context via MCP servers.

features.title: Multi-Model Support
features.description: Switch between Claude, GPT, and Windsurf's own in-house SWE coding model depending on task and cost.

features.title: Terminal Command Execution
features.description: Cascade can run shell commands such as installing dependencies or running migrations as part of its plan.

features.title: VS Code-Based Editor
features.description: Built on a familiar Visual Studio Code fork, keeping keybindings, themes, and much of the extension experience intact.

features.title: Enterprise Deployment Options
features.description: Self-hosted and air-gapped deployment options carried over from the product's Codeium enterprise roots.

features.title: Extensions and Integrations
features.description: Integrations with tools like Slack, Linear, Figma, and common language servers to extend the editor's workflow.

pros: Cascade agent handles multi-file, multi-step coding tasks with reviewable diffs
Fast, unlimited autocomplete even on the free plan
Flexible Model Context Protocol support for connecting external tools and data
Access to multiple frontier AI models plus an in-house coding model
Backed by Cognition's broader Devin agent ecosystem and engineering resources
cons: Product has been renamed twice (Codeium to Windsurf to Devin Desktop), creating brand confusion
Pricing structure has changed more than once, from credits to daily/weekly quotas
Future roadmap now depends on Cognition's Devin strategy rather than a standalone Windsurf team
Higher usage tiers (Max plan) can get expensive for heavy daily agent use
Enterprise customers must track migration and login changes tied to the rebrand

faqs.question: Is Windsurf still called Windsurf?
faqs.answer: As of June 2026, Cognition rebranded the Windsurf editor as Devin Desktop. Existing Windsurf accounts, plans, and settings carried over automatically, and Legacy Windsurf Enterprise customers continue to log in via windsurf.com/enterprise.

faqs.question: What happened after Cognition acquired Windsurf?
faqs.answer: Cognition, the maker of the autonomous coding agent Devin, acquired Windsurf in 2025 for a reported figure around 250 million dollars, then folded the product into its Devin family, renaming it Devin Desktop in June 2026.

faqs.question: Does Windsurf have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. The free tier includes unlimited Tab-style autocomplete and inline edits, plus a limited daily and weekly quota for Cascade agent tasks.

faqs.question: What AI models does Windsurf support?
faqs.answer: Windsurf supports multiple frontier models, including models from Anthropic and OpenAI, alongside its own in-house SWE coding model, selectable depending on the task and plan.

faqs.question: Does Windsurf support the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cascade natively supports MCP, letting teams connect their own external tools, servers, and data sources into the agent's context.

use_cases.title: Multi-file refactoring
use_cases.description: Use Cascade to plan and execute changes across many files at once, reviewing each staged diff before it is applied.

use_cases.title: Rapid prototyping
use_cases.description: Describe a feature in plain language and let Cascade scaffold files, install dependencies, and iterate quickly toward a working prototype.

use_cases.title: Enterprise codebase modernization
use_cases.description: Apply agent-assisted changes across large, self-hosted codebases while keeping human review and approval in the loop.

use_cases.title: Learning AI-assisted development
use_cases.description: Explore how an AI agent reasons about code, proposes edits, and runs commands, with every step visible for learning purposes.
use_cases.audience: Students and developers new to AI coding tools

features: Unlimited Tab-style autocomplete and inline edits
Limited daily and weekly Cascade agent quota
Higher daily and weekly Cascade quotas
Access to premium models including Claude and GPT models
Access to Windsurf's in-house SWE coding model

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Windsurf (now Devin Desktop) is an agentic AI code editor with the Cascade agent, MCP support, and multi-model autocomplete.

enrichment.short_description: Wise Business lets companies hold, send, and receive 40+ currencies with transparent, mid-market exchange rates and no monthly fee.
enrichment.starting_price: $31 one-time setup fee; transaction fees from around 0.33%

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Wise Business is the business account product from Wise (formerly TransferWise), the UK-founded fintech that pioneered sending international transfers at the mid-market exchange rate for consumers before extending the same model to companies. Founded in 2011 in London by Kristo Käärmann and Taavet Hinrikus, the company rebranded from TransferWise to Wise in 2021 and went public that same year via a direct listing on the London Stock Exchange, and now operates in the vast majority of countries worldwide, with Wise Business built specifically for companies that send, receive, hold, or convert money in multiple currencies.

At its core, Wise Business is a multi-currency account: businesses can hold balances in dozens of currencies, receive local account details, such as routing numbers, IBANs, and sort codes, in several major currencies, and convert between them using the mid-market exchange rate plus a small, disclosed fee rather than the wider spreads typical of traditional banks. There is no monthly subscription for the core account; instead, customers pay a one-time setup fee once and then per-transaction fees tied to how much they actually send, convert, or withdraw.

The product bundles currency conversion, payments, card issuing, batch payouts, invoicing, and accounting-software integrations into a single web dashboard and mobile app, positioning itself less as a full replacement for a traditional business bank and more as the international payments and multi-currency treasury layer that sits alongside, or in some cases instead of, a local business bank account for companies with cross-border operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Wise Business Is For
paragraphs: Wise Business is built for freelancers, sole traders, and small-to-medium businesses that regularly send, receive, or hold money across borders, from e-commerce sellers paying overseas suppliers to marketing agencies invoicing clients in the US, UK, or EU.

It is also a natural fit for startups, agencies, and remote-first companies that pay contractors or distributed teams in multiple countries, since batch payments let a business upload a spreadsheet and pay hundreds of people in one go rather than initiating each transfer individually.

Marketplaces, importers and exporters, and consultancies that want local receiving details in currencies such as USD, EUR, GBP, or AUD, so they look like a local business to their counterparties, are a strong match, as are any company looking for a lower-cost, more transparent alternative to their bank's wire fees and hidden exchange-rate markups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Companies that need traditional lending products, such as business loans, overdrafts, credit lines, or merchant cash advances, will not find them here, since Wise does not offer credit or financing.

Businesses that handle significant physical cash, need in-branch banking, or require sophisticated treasury features like cash pooling, sweeping, or multi-entity consolidation may find Wise's toolset too lightweight for their operations.

Larger enterprises that need a dedicated relationship manager, bespoke banking terms, or extensive integration with legacy ERP systems, and companies operating in higher-risk categories Wise does not support, will typically be better served by a conventional commercial bank or a specialized banking-as-a-service provider built for their industry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: The core of Wise Business is its multi-currency balance, letting companies hold and convert dozens of currencies in one account and get local account details, routing numbers, IBANs, sort codes, in several major currencies, so clients abroad can pay as if paying a local business.

Payment tools include batch payments for paying up to 1,000 recipients in a single upload, invoicing and shareable payment links for collecting customer payments, and Wise multi-currency cards for team spending, with admin controls for setting individual spending limits and permissions across a business's team members.

For developers and finance teams, Wise offers a public API for automating payments, payouts, and reconciliation at scale, alongside native integrations with accounting platforms such as Xero and QuickBooks that automatically sync transactions into the right ledgers and currencies.

Balances in certain currencies can also earn variable interest, and all transfers run on the mid-market exchange rate with a disclosed percentage fee rather than a bank's typical spread, giving finance teams a clearer picture of the true cost of moving money internationally.

Wise is a regulated financial institution, or operates through regulated partners, in the jurisdictions it serves, and safeguards customer funds separately from its own operating funds rather than lending them out, details that matter for finance teams evaluating a non-bank provider for handling company funds.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Wise Business Experience
paragraphs: Signing up is done entirely online: a business submits company and ownership details, completes identity and compliance verification, and can typically start using the account within a few days once approved, without needing to visit a branch or meet with a banker.

Day-to-day, the web dashboard and mobile apps for iOS and Android give a real-time view of balances across currencies, transaction history, team spending, and integrations, with transfers initiated in a few clicks and most international payments arriving in well under a day.

Fee transparency is a consistent theme throughout the product, costs are shown upfront before a transfer is confirmed, and customer support is available via in-app chat and help center articles rather than phone-first banking, which suits digitally-native teams but may feel less personal than a traditional business banking relationship.

There is no cheque or cash deposit facility and no physical branch network, so businesses that occasionally need to handle cash will need a separate banking relationship for that purpose, but for a company whose finances are already digital, the experience feels closer to a modern SaaS product than a legacy bank portal.

features.title: Multi-currency account
features.description: Hold and manage balances in dozens of currencies from a single account instead of opening separate foreign accounts.

features.title: Local account details
features.description: Get local bank details, such as IBAN, routing number, or sort code, in several major currencies to receive payments like a local business.

features.title: Batch payments
features.description: Upload a file to pay up to 1,000 recipients in one go, useful for payroll, contractor payouts, or supplier payments.

features.title: Wise Business card
features.description: Issue multi-currency debit cards to employees with individual spending limits and real-time expense tracking.

features.title: Invoicing and payment links
features.description: Create invoices and shareable payment links so international customers can pay directly into the account.

features.title: Mid-market exchange rates
features.description: Convert currencies at the real mid-market rate plus a small, disclosed fee instead of a hidden bank markup.

features.title: Accounting software integrations
features.description: Sync transactions automatically with platforms such as Xero and QuickBooks to cut down manual bookkeeping.

features.title: API access
features.description: Automate payments, payouts, and reconciliation programmatically through Wise's business API.

features.title: Interest on balances
features.description: Earn variable interest on eligible currency balances held in the account.

features.title: Web and mobile access
features.description: Manage the account from a web dashboard or the iOS and Android apps with real-time balance visibility.

pros: Mid-market exchange rates with transparent, disclosed fees instead of hidden bank markups
No monthly account fee; pay-as-you-go pricing based on actual usage
Local receiving account details in multiple currencies make it easy for international clients to pay without wire fees
Batch payments and API access streamline payouts for teams handling contractors or suppliers at scale
Fast international transfers, with most arriving well within a day
cons: One-time setup fee applies even for very small or occasional international senders
No lending, overdraft, or credit products, so it cannot replace a full-service business bank
No physical branches or cash deposit facilities
ATM withdrawals beyond the free monthly allowance and e-wallet top-ups carry additional fees
Customer support is primarily digital, via chat and help center, without phone-first banking for urgent issues

faqs.question: Does Wise Business charge a monthly fee?
faqs.answer: No, Wise Business has no recurring monthly subscription. Customers pay a one-time setup fee and then per-transaction fees based on usage, such as currency conversion, transfers, and card spending.

faqs.question: Which currencies can I hold in a Wise Business account?
faqs.answer: Wise Business supports holding and converting dozens of currencies, with local account details available in several major currencies, such as USD, EUR, GBP, and AUD, so businesses can receive payments like a local company.

faqs.question: Can I pay multiple people at once with Wise Business?
faqs.answer: Yes, batch payments let businesses upload a file and pay up to 1,000 recipients in a single transaction, which is useful for payroll, contractor payouts, or supplier payments.

faqs.question: Does Wise Business integrate with accounting software?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wise Business connects natively with platforms such as Xero and QuickBooks, automatically syncing transactions so businesses do not have to enter multi-currency transfers manually.

faqs.question: Is Wise Business regulated and safe for holding company funds?
faqs.answer: Wise operates as a regulated financial institution, or through regulated partners, in the markets it serves and safeguards customer funds separately from its own operating funds, though it is not a traditional bank and works differently from bank deposit protections.

use_cases.title: Paying international contractors and remote teams
use_cases.description: A company with remote employees or freelancers in different countries uses batch payments to pay everyone in their local currency from a single upload, cutting down on the time and fees involved in individual wire transfers.

use_cases.title: Collecting payments from overseas customers
use_cases.description: An e-commerce or SaaS business uses local account details in USD, EUR, and GBP so international customers can pay via local bank transfer, avoiding the wire fees and delays of cross-border payments.

use_cases.title: Managing multi-currency supplier payments
