use_cases.description: An importer converts revenue into a supplier's local currency at the mid-market rate and sends payment directly, reducing the margin lost to a traditional bank's exchange-rate markup.

use_cases.title: Controlling team expenses across borders
use_cases.description: A growing startup issues Wise Business cards to employees traveling or spending internationally, setting individual limits and tracking spend in real time from the dashboard.
use_cases.audience: Startups and scale-ups with distributed teams

features: Currency conversion from around 0.33% at the mid-market rate
Local receiving account details in multiple currencies
Batch payments and invoicing tools included
Available to businesses converting large monthly volumes
Lower FX conversion fees than the standard tier
Same core account features as Pay As You Go
Support suited to higher-volume payment needs

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Wise Business: a multi-currency account for international payments, local receiving details, batch payouts, cards, and accounting integrations.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source WordPress ecommerce plugin from Automattic for building and fully owning a customizable online store.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (core plugin); premium extensions from about $29/year, hosting and payment processing billed separately
enrichment.company_size: 1,000-5,000 employees (Automattic, parent company; fully distributed workforce)
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, USA (Automattic; operates as a fully distributed remote company)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: WooCommerce is a free, open-source ecommerce plugin for WordPress that turns any WordPress website into a fully functional online store. It launched in 2011, built by the WooThemes team of Mark Forrester, Adii Pienaar, and Magnus Jepson, and was acquired by Automattic, the company behind WordPress.com and Jetpack, in 2015. It was later rebranded simply as Woo.

Because it is released under the GPL and distributed through WordPress.org, WooCommerce carries no licensing fee, no mandatory monthly subscription, and no revenue share on sales. Store owners install it on self-hosted WordPress, keep full ownership of their data, and choose their own hosting, payment processors, and themes rather than being locked into a single vendor's infrastructure.

With millions of active installations, WooCommerce is one of the most widely used ecommerce platforms on the web, powering everything from small hobby shops to large B2B catalogs. Its block-based product editor, native checkout blocks, and growing extensions marketplace let merchants add payments, shipping, subscriptions, bookings, and marketing tools as a store grows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who WooCommerce Is For
paragraphs: WooCommerce suits anyone already running, or planning to run, a WordPress site who wants to add a store without switching platforms. Bloggers, publishers, and content creators can sell physical products, digital downloads, or memberships alongside their existing content, blending commerce into a site they already control.

It's a strong fit for small and medium businesses that want to avoid platform transaction fees and retain full control over design, data, and hosting. Agencies and freelance developers also favor WooCommerce because its open codebase, action hooks, and REST API make heavily customized or white-labeled storefronts straightforward to build.

Merchants with unique product types, such as rentals, appointments, subscriptions, or wholesale and B2B catalogs, benefit from the deep extension ecosystem that lets them assemble a store tailored to a specific business model rather than working within a fixed set of platform features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Non-technical users who want a fully managed, zero-maintenance store bundled into one predictable subscription may find a closed SaaS platform like Shopify a simpler starting point, since WooCommerce requires assembling hosting, security, and updates yourself, or paying someone else to do it.

Merchants who dislike managing a WordPress site, including plugin updates, theme compatibility, and occasional conflicts between extensions, should be cautious. WooCommerce's flexibility comes with more moving parts to maintain compared to a closed, all-in-one platform where the vendor handles infrastructure.

Very high-traffic or complex enterprise catalogs can outgrow a standard WordPress and WooCommerce stack without meaningful performance engineering, caching, and dedicated hosting, so businesses expecting instant, out-of-the-box enterprise-grade scaling may prefer a platform built primarily around scale from day one.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, WooCommerce provides product and inventory management through the WordPress block editor, flexible cart and checkout blocks, and support for physical goods, digital downloads, and variable products, all customizable through standard WordPress themes.

Built-in and extension-based tools cover payments through WooPayments and gateways like Stripe, PayPal, and Square, shipping label printing with live carrier rates, automated tax calculation, and marketing features such as coupons, abandoned cart recovery, and email marketing integrations.

Official extensions add subscriptions, bookings, and memberships, while the WooCommerce mobile app lets store owners track orders, manage products, and view analytics on iOS and Android. A REST API also supports headless storefronts and custom integrations for developers.

long_form_blocks.heading: The WooCommerce Experience
paragraphs: Getting started typically means installing WordPress, adding the WooCommerce plugin, and running its setup wizard to configure store details, payments, shipping, and tax, a process that's approachable for existing WordPress users but involves more upfront configuration than a fully hosted competitor.

Day-to-day, store owners manage everything from the familiar WordPress dashboard, with a large ecosystem of themes, extensions, and third-party developers available to fill gaps. The tradeoff is that quality and support vary by extension, and larger stores often need a developer for performance tuning or custom features.

Community support is extensive, spanning official documentation, the WooCommerce.com marketplace, community forums, and a large pool of WordPress developers and agencies, which keeps help accessible even though there is no single dedicated account manager built into every plan the way there might be on a closed SaaS platform.

features.title: Product & Catalog Management
features.description: Manage physical goods, digital downloads, and variable products through the native WordPress block editor with categories, attributes, and variations.

features.title: Cart & Checkout Blocks
features.description: Customizable, native checkout and cart blocks that can be styled and rearranged to match any WordPress theme.

features.title: WooPayments
features.description: Built-in payment processing alongside support for third-party gateways such as Stripe, PayPal, and Square.

features.title: Shipping Tools
features.description: Shipping zones, live carrier rates, and label printing to handle domestic and international fulfillment.

features.title: Tax Automation
features.description: Automated tax calculation across regions to reduce manual rate configuration for merchants.

features.title: Subscriptions & Memberships
features.description: Official extensions add recurring billing, membership content gating, and appointment or rental bookings.

features.title: Extensions Marketplace
features.description: A large catalog of official and third-party extensions for marketing, shipping, accounting, and more.

features.title: Mobile App
features.description: iOS and Android apps for tracking orders, managing products, and viewing store stats on the go.

features.title: REST API
features.description: A documented REST API that supports headless storefronts and custom integrations with other systems.

features.title: Store Analytics
features.description: Built-in analytics dashboard for tracking sales, orders, and customer activity over time.

pros: Free, open-source core with no mandatory monthly platform fee or revenue share
Full ownership of store data, code, and hosting environment
Deep customization through WordPress themes, hooks, and a large extension ecosystem
Scales from a simple shop to complex subscription and B2B catalogs
Backed by a large WordPress developer community and extension marketplace
cons: Requires self-managed hosting, security, and plugin or theme updates
Total cost can add up once hosting, extensions, and premium themes are combined
Performance depends heavily on hosting quality and plugin compatibility
Steeper learning curve than fully hosted platforms for non-technical users
Support quality varies across third-party extensions and themes

faqs.question: Is WooCommerce really free?
faqs.answer: The core WooCommerce plugin is free and open source, but most stores also pay for hosting, a domain, and often premium themes or extensions, so total cost varies by setup.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to use WooCommerce?
faqs.answer: No coding is required for a basic store setup, but customizing design or adding advanced functionality typically benefits from developer help.

faqs.question: What payment gateways does WooCommerce support?
faqs.answer: WooCommerce supports its own WooPayments processor as well as third-party gateways including Stripe, PayPal, Square, and many regional options.

faqs.question: Can WooCommerce handle large or high-traffic stores?
faqs.answer: Yes, with appropriate hosting, caching, and performance optimization, WooCommerce can support large catalogs and high traffic, though this requires more setup than a fully hosted platform.

faqs.question: How is WooCommerce different from Shopify?
faqs.answer: WooCommerce is a self-hosted, open-source plugin that runs on your own WordPress site with no revenue share, while Shopify is a fully hosted SaaS platform that manages infrastructure for you.

use_cases.title: Content-to-Commerce Blogs
use_cases.description: Bloggers and content creators add a shop or digital downloads alongside their existing WordPress content without switching platforms.

use_cases.title: Small Business Retail Stores
use_cases.description: Independent retailers run a self-hosted online store with full control over branding, data, and payment processing.

use_cases.title: Subscription & Membership Businesses
use_cases.description: Brands sell recurring products or gated content using subscription and membership extensions built on WooCommerce.

use_cases.title: Agency-Built Custom Storefronts
use_cases.description: Developers and agencies use WooCommerce's open codebase and REST API to build bespoke or headless storefronts for clients.

features: Full customization via themes and hooks
Integrated card and digital wallet payments
Access to premium and store themes

enrichment.seo_meta_description: WooCommerce: the free, open-source WordPress ecommerce plugin from Automattic. See features, pricing, pros, cons, and FAQs before building your store.
